textPlain: They stopped accepting new users ~5 years ago, so it's hardly a surprise... but I'm still bummed to see this.Even so, 22 years is a good run!
They're using the phrases "deactivated" and "not available to you" a number of times. No mentions of "delete" or "removed" on the page.
Sad news.Typepad brings backs fond memories of early personal "weblog", Web 1.0/2.0 era, Six Apart & Movable Type.
Kind of interesting that, with such an entrenched service that seems highly automatable, this is preferable to just keeping it in maintenance mode.
Lot of content here that will go down with the ship. Hope the Internet Archive will mirror it.
September 30 is a pretty small window to migrate. Hopefully it's enough.
They should provide an option to move to https://ghost.org/
Was this just not profitable? Or not vc extra growth profitable?
I initially thought that the 30ish day notice was too low, but that definitely softens it.

I was thinking the same thing. "Deactivated" is different from "deleted."I’m not a customer, but in today’s world, I would actually prefer that when the service shuts down, all accounts and published data are destroyed. Just wiped completely. Otherwise, what are the odds that customer PII gets sold off and the service owner licenses the previously hosted posts and comments to an AI company?
At least with LLMs, we can just write a query to migrate the export to whatever target format we want. The main issue is just breaking 20 years worth of inbound links.

I have no doubt that it wasn't vc profitable, but my assumption (without any inside info) is that the real issue is that they were using some hacked up version of Movable Type that they couldn't upgrade.It's frustrating though because imho it's arguably still the best blog platform to this day.
Does anyone read blogs anymore? Social media seems to have completely replaced them.
